brief: GAP/RATIONALE: Wider circumferential PV isolation that captures a large portion of left atrial posterior wall (LAPW) is thought beneficial; unknown how much extra isolation area is achievable by inflating a visually guided laser balloon (VGLB) to its largest size vs conventional size. DESIGN: Prospective single-arm study; each patient acts as own control (paired before/after comparison of two balloon inflation sizes). POPULATION: 78 patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) undergoing VGLB ablation; includes both paroxysmal and persistent AF. PROCEDURE/COMPARISON: Step 1 = pulmonary vein isolation (PVI) with conventional-sized VGLB; electroanatomic LA map taken before and after. Step 2 = isolation area extended using largest-sized VGLB, then remapped same way. Isolation areas computed with CARTO-3 system. Endpoint = 1-year atrial arrhythmia (Ata) recurrence/freedom. RESULTS (all conventional vs largest-size, mean±SD): Left-sided PV antrum (PVA) isolation area 11.5±2.3 cm2 vs 15.9±3.5 cm2, P<.001. Right-sided PVA isolation area 14.2±3.3 cm2 vs 20.6±4.4 cm2, P<.001. Non-ablated LAPW area 12.3±4.4 cm2 vs 7.8±3.9 cm2 (i.e., reduced with largest-size), P<.001. One-year Ata freedom: 83.7% in paroxysmal AF, 96.4% in persistent AF. TAKEAWAY: Largest-sized VGLB ablation creates significantly wider PVA isolation and debulks substantially more LAPW than conventional-sized VGLB; 1-year freedom from atrial arrhythmia was similarly high in paroxysmal and persistent AF.
